coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for high selective detecting p-nitrophenol through FRET mechanism
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Sen
Ding, Zui
Wang, Shuo
Tan, Fangyu
Ge, Yi
Cui, Yaqing
Tan, Ni
Wang, Hongqing
coredata.dc:description: A facile, friendly and one-step hydrothermal protocol was used to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by utilizing hexamethylenetetramine and ethanediamine as the carbon and nitrogen sources. It demonstrated good water solubility and fluorescence properties were stable, whether in acidic or alkaline. Quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was 8.3% at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm with maximum emission at 425 nm. The fluorescence of N-CDs achieved very high fluorescence quenching of 60% in the detection of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) in aqueous medium via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanisms. Under optimum conditions, fluorescence probs of N-CDs had strong selectivity to p-NP, and the fluorescence intensity was linearly proportional to p-NP concentration from 0.5 to 70.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.201 μM. The corresponding cell experiments were also performed, indicating that the prepared N-CDs possessed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Meanwhile, the N-CDs can be used for the determination of p-NP in river water and industrial wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent detection
p-nitrophenol
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence analysis of p-NP in aqueous solution
Detection of p-NP in practical sample
Characterization of the N-CDs and optical properties

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of N-CDs and fluorescence analysis of p-NP.
(A) TEM images of N-CDs. (B) Emission spectra under various excitation wavelengths that range from 305 nm to 345 nm. (C) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs. (D) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D).

(A) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 325 nm) of N-CDs in aqueous solution at pH values from 4 to 10. (B) Effect of ionic strengths on fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with various concentrations of NaCl (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 M).

(a) Selectivity analyses of fluorescent probe N-CDs (black) and the solution of N-CDs and p-NP (red) mixed various interfering chemicals, respectively; (B) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of increasing p-NP concentrations (0–70.0 μM) in 0.01 M PBS at pH 7.0; (C) The relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and p-NP from 10.0 to 70.0 μM; (D) Plot of (F-F0)/F0 of N-CDs vs concentration of p-NP from 0.5 to 10.0 μM; (F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 425 nm in the presence or absence of p-NP, respectively).
(A) UV-vis absorption (a) of p-NP, excitation (b) and emission (c). (B) Photoluminescence decay trace of N-CDs in the presence and absence of p-NP.
Fluorescence microscopy images for HeLa cells. (A) represent the bright-field images of HeLa cell. (B) Images of cells incubated with N-CDs. (C) Overlaid fluorescence images of HeLa cells.
Comparison of the analytical performance of the sensors for p-NP determination.
Determination of p-NP in practical water samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for high selective detecting p-nitrophenol through FRET mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang, Hunan Province 421001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang, Hunan Province 421001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The N-CDs were prepared using hexamethylenetetramine as carbon sources and ethanolamine acted as nitrogen sources. The p-NP can quench the fluorescence of N-CDs through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism.

A facile, friendly and one-step hydrothermal protocol was used to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by utilizing hexamethylenetetramine and ethanediamine as the carbon and nitrogen sources. It demonstrated good water solubility and fluorescence properties were stable, whether in acidic or alkaline. Quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was 8.3% at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm with maximum emission at 425 nm. The fluorescence of N-CDs achieved very high fluorescence quenching of 60% in the detection of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) in aqueous medium via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanisms. Under optimum conditions, fluorescence probs of N-CDs had strong selectivity to p-NP, and the fluorescence intensity was linearly proportional to p-NP concentration from 0.5 to 70.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.201 μM. The corresponding cell experiments were also performed, indicating that the prepared N-CDs possessed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Meanwhile, the N-CDs can be used for the determination of p-NP in river water and industrial wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: p-nitrophenol (p-NP) is part of the nitrophenols cited in the list of priority pollutants of the U.S.A. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) due to its toxic pollutant and duration [1,2]. However, because it is usually used as an intermediate for fine chemicals, such as pesticides, drugs, dyes and leather processing [3,4]. If we don't deal with waste in the chemical industry seriously, it may lead to serious environmental pollution. Moreover, because of the nitro and phenol functionalities, they cause intense irritation on the skin and eyes [5]. There are three isomers of the nitrophenol, the p-NP represent the most persistent and highly toxic nitrophenols [6,7], Therefore, the detection of p-NP is of great significance with high precision and ultra sensitivity [8].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of nanostructured carbon materials [9–12], which possess excellent optical properties, so it is widely used in fluorescence detection [13–15], photocatalysis [16] and bioimaging [17,18]. And as a fluorescence sensor used to detect metal ions [19], small organic molecules [20–22], antibiotics [23], and so on. Therefore, Li et al. synthesis of chromium(III)-doped carbon dots (Cr-CDs) to sensitively p-NP [24]. Qu et al. prepare carbon dots (CDs), as a sensor exhibited sensitivity detection for o-nitrophenol (o-NP), m-nitrophenol (m-NP) and p-NP [1]. However, there is no selectivity to p-NP. On the other hand, some methods involve interference during detection, the R-CDs [25] using molecular imprinting method, but the preparation of probe is more complicated. So there is an urgent need to develop more selective and facile fluorescent probes for p-NP.

In this work, an efficient aqueous synthesis of N-CDs was proposed using hexamethylenetetramine and ethanediamine as reaction precursors via one step hydrothermal method. The synthesized N-CDs had uniform morphology, excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior, remarkable water-solubility, high ionic strength and pH dependence. Remarkably, we find that N-CDs could be directly utilized as rapid and highly selective sensors for p-NP detection without any functional modification. Moreover, their application in biological was also investigated through MTT assay and fluorescence imaging on HeLa cells. On the other hand, the p-NP can quench the fluorescence of N-CDs through the FRET mechanism, due to the overlap of the absorption spectrum of p-NP with the excitation spectrum of N-CDs and the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs changed when adding p-NP. Finally, the N-CDs were successfully applied to detect p-NP in river water and industrial wastewater.

N-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal technique using hexamethylenetetramine and ethanediamine in PTFE autoclave. 420 mg of hexamethylenetetramine and 0.34 mL of ethanediamine were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL PTFE autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 5 h. Dark brown solution was obtained after cooled down to room temperature. And then the solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3500 Da) for 24 h while ultrapure water was changed with an interval of 8 h. Finally, N-CDs were freeze dried to brown solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of p-NP in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis of p-NP was performed under the following methods. In brief, 100 μL of N-CDs solution (50 μg mL−1) was mixed with various concentrations of p-NP solution, and then the mixed solution was diluted to 1 mL with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7). The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm and emission wavelength at 420 nm after shaken for 1 min.

The potential of N-CDs in bio-labeling was preliminarily evaluated by their fluorescence imaging in Hela cells. In brief, an initial density of 1 × 104 cells mL−1 was seeded in each hole and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h under a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The dispersion of N-CDs was prepared in DMEM medium with a concentration of 50 μg mL−1. The Hela cells were cultured with the dispersion of N-CDs for 12 h and washed three times with PBS, and then imaged on Nikon TE2000 inverted fluorescent microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of p-NP in practical sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to research the possibility of practical application of the synthesized fluorescence N-CDs (50 μg mL−1), the practical water samples were collected from river water and industrial wastewater. The practical water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to eliminate large particulate matter. First of all, river water and industrial wastewater were spiked with different concentrations (1.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μM) of standard p-NP solution. Then 100 μL of the N-CDs solution mixed with 100 μL of the above sample solutions was diluted to 1 mL with PBS buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7). The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm and emission wavelength at 420 nm after shaken for 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the N-CDs and optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, hexamethylenetetramine and ethanediamine were selected as raw materials for preparing carbon dots by hydrothermal method. The N-CDs were prepared using hexamethylenetetramine and ethanolamine acted as carbon sources and nitrogen sources (Fig. S1). The fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by adding p-NP. The QY of N-CDs was calculated to be 8.3% by using quinine sulfate as the reference standard [26] (Fig. S2).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of N-CDs are shown in Fig. 1
A, which reveals that N-CDs have good dispersibility and a near-spherical morphology, the average particle size is nearly 2.53 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). As displayed in the HRTEM images (Fig. S3), one nanoparticle reveals that N-CDs has a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon as reported [27]. In the excitation range between 305 nm and 345 nm, the emission behavior of N-CDs remained centered at 425 nm (Fig. 1B). The shift emission peaks with an excitation wavelength of 305 nm to 345 nm displayed the reabsorption of itself in the shorter wavelength region. This stable blue emission is ascribed to the electronic transition in topological defect states of N-CDs. The excitation is dependent on emission in longer wavelength regions, which is probably due to sp2 hybridization of carbon clusters with size effect [28,29]. To identify the chemical structure of N-CDs, FT-IR spectroscopy was performed. As it can be seen in Fig. 1C, a broad peak at 3313 cm−1 correlating with stretching vibrations of NH or OH. The band around 2967 and 2875 cm−1 can be attributed to the CH of methylene and formyl groups, respectively. The other peaks at 1663, 1415, 1276, 1088 and 1052 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of CO, CNC, CN, COC and CO, respectively [30,31]. In Fig. 1D, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs shows that the absorption peak at 320 nm indicated the n-π*transition of CO [32,33].

The XPS measurements were used to investigate the chemical bonds and element composition in the surface of N-CDs. The XPS spectrum (Fig. S4A) shows three peaks at 285.08 eV, 399.08 eV and 532.08 eV, which are attributed to C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively [34]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s has four peaks at 284.2, 284.8, 285.6 and 286.7 eV (Fig. 4 B) belongs to CC, CN, CO and CO functional groups, respectively [33,35]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s has two peaks at 398.9 and 400.3 eV (Fig. S4C) belong to CN and NH functional groups, respectively [36,37]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s has two peaks at 532.1 and 533.4 eV (Fig. S4D) belong to CO and CO functional groups, respectively [38,39].

It was widely known that detection conditions such as pH and ion strength were not ideal in actual application. So the effect of pH and ion strength of the background solvent on the FL intensity of the N-CDs was studied. We can find that the FL intensity of the N-CDs had changed just a little with pH from 4 to 10, and the optimal pH is 7.0 (Fig. S5A). The FL intensity was stable with 0-–2.0 M NaCl solution concentration (Fig. S5B), indicating that N-CDs had excellent fluorescence stability. On the other hand, response times were studied, the results show (Fig. S5C) that the p-NP induced fluorescence quenching of the fluorescence of the N-CDs could complete within 0.5 min, allowing the sensor with fast response. To sum up, the N-CDs could be applied to a complex practical environment due to good fluorescence stability.

To evaluate the selectivity of N-CDs fluorescent probes to p-NP, and the N-CDs relative fluorescence intensities were researched when the presence of metal ions and organic compounds including Co2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, o-NP, m-NP, p-hydroquinone (p-HQ), p-phenylenediamine (p-PD), cysteine (L-Cys) and ascorbic acid (AA) under the same experimental conditions (100 μM interferents). As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A (black bar), the FL intensity decreased when p-NP was introduced to the N-CDs composite solution. And the quenching of p-NP (60.3%) was nearly three times of o-NP (22.1%) and m-NP (22.8%), verifying the selectivity of the N-CDs to the compounds with similar properties. On the other hand, to examine the anti-interference ability of p-NP system detected by fluorescent N-CDs, the above metal ions and organic compounds were added to the detection system. As shown in Fig. 5A (red bar), there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity of the solution of N-CDs and p-NP, indicating that the detection system would not be disturbed by other metal ions and organic compounds. In summary, fluorescent probes of N-CDs have a high selectivity of p-NP.

As shown in Fig. 5B, the N-CDs fluorescence intensity around 425 nm was decreased as the p-NP concentration increased range from 0.1 to 70.0 μM. The (F0 − F)/F0 exhibited relationships with the concentration of p-NP in the range of 0.5–70.0 μM (Fig. 5C). and exhibited linear relationship (F0 − F)/F0 = 0.00637 [p-NP] (μM) + 0.02648 (R2 = 0.994) with the concentration of p-NP in the range of 10.0–70.0 μM. And it's another linear relationship at low concentrations (0.5–10 μM), as shown in Fig. 5D, with a calibration equation as (F0 − F)/F0 = 0.00927 [p-NP] (μM) + 0.0033 (R2 = 0.997), the detection limit (LOD) of p-NP was 0.201 μM based on the equation: LOD = 3σ/S (σ is standard deviation of background recorded (N = 10) and S is the slope of the linear fit). As shown in Table 1 , a comparison of fluorescence detection time that N-CDs only need 30 s, compared to the maximum time of eight minutes for other methods. On the other hand, most of the methods involve interference during detection, the R-CDs using molecular imprinting method, the preparation of probe is more complicated. To sum up, the N-CDs offer a more rapid, highly selective, and facile detection process that does not require sophisticated pretreatment procedures. Meanwhile, the LOD is lower than 0.431 μM (60 ng/mL) set by the US EPA for drinking water, signifying that the N-CDs can be applied to analyzing p-NP contained in practical water samples.

In addition, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CDs fluorescent probes was researched. It had been reported that the quenching mechanisms of quencher to fluorescence carbon dots mainly include dynamic quenching (DQ), static quenching (SQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [43,44].

Firstly, as shown in Fig. 6 A, the absorbance peak of p-NP (a) had an obvious overlap with the emission spectrum of N-CDs (c), indicating that there was a FRET or IFE between the N-CDs and p-NP [2,45]. To further investigate the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs before and after adding p-NP was detected, as shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence lifetime was 10.47 ns and 7.80 ns, respectively. The donor–acceptor distance (γ) was calculated through Eqs. (1)–(3) [32]. Finally, the γ between the N-CDs and p-NP was calculated to be 14 Å in the range of 10–100 Å, consistent with requirements for a FRET mechanism [44].
(1)
E = 1 -
τ 0
/ τ
(2)
R 0
= 0.211
κ
2
n
- 4
∅ J
λ
1 / 6
( i n
Å
u n i t )
(3)
E = 1 /

γ /
R 0
6

Å
u n i t )
where τ0 and τ were the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs in the absence and in the presence of p-NP, κ2 was the orientation factor of the donor and acceptor transition dipoles and was typically assumed to be 2/3, Φ is the quantum yield of the N-CDs, n was the refractive index of the medium, and J(λ) was the integral of overlap values.

N-CDs had good detection performance and good practical application prospect, so biotoxicity and compatibility should be evaluated. The biotoxicity of the N-CDs fluorescence probe was evaluated using HeLa cells through MTT assays. As shown in Fig. S3, over 90% cell viability was observed after 24 h incubation of the HeLa cells with the N-CDs (0.1–100 µg mL−1). The result indicates that the N-CDs fluorescence probe had low biotoxicity and great potential applications in the fluorescence biometric.

Fluorescence imaging of the N-CDs was tested on HeLa cells, under the confocal laser scanning microscope, we see the results shown in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7A, the images of HeLa cells incubated with N-CDs (100 μg mL−1) for 1.0 h under bright field. The HeLa cells show bright fluorescence under blue channel (Fig. 7B). All these results indicated that the N-CDs had potential applications in biotechnology field owing to their low biotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

To study the feasibility and practicability of the new N-CDs fluorescence probes, the standard addition method (1.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μM) was adopted for the assay of p-NP content in river water and industrial wastewater. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery of this method was ranging from 98.0% to 106.7%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) to use this method was less than 3%. Exemplify that the fluorescence probes of N-CDs could be used to detect p-NP in practical samples.

To sum up, the N-CDs fluorescent probe was successfully synthesized by one-step hydrothermal protocol using hexamethylenetetramine and ethanediamine as precursors. The N-CDs not only displayed superior dispersibility and excellent stability but also exhibited good selective response toward p-NP with a detection limit of 0.201 μM. The N-CDs fluorescent probe possessed a wide linear range (0.5–70.0 μM). We found that the p-NP could quench the fluorescence of N-CDs through the FRET mechanism through mechanism study. Moreover, the N-CDs fluorescent probe had good biological compatibility and low biotoxicity. The N-CDs fluorescent probe also had a potential application detection of p-NP in practical samples.

Sen Liao: Formulation of overarching research goals and aims, Performing the experiments, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zui Ding: Conducting a research and investigation process. Shuo Wang: Preparation, Performing the experiments. Fangyu Tan: Software, Validation. Yi Ge: Investigation, Data curation. Yaqing Cui: Investigation, Supervision. Ni Tan: Writing - review & editing. Hongqing Wang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of the University of South China [190XQD104], and the Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department [20C1609] in China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119897.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
